title: OAuth 2.0 — Device Authorization Grant
description: The Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628) solves authorization for input-constrained devices like CLIs, smart TVs, media boxes, and IoT sensors that cannot open a browser or receive redirects. The device obtains a user code and verification URI from the AS, displays them to the user, and polls for a token while the user authorizes on a separate device (phone or laptop).

steps.label: POST /device_authorization

artifacts.signing_key_hint: No user interaction at this step — device initiates the flow

steps.label: 1. device_code — the device's session identifier
steps.value:
device_code = "GmRhmhcxhwAzkoEqiMEg_DnyEysNkuNhszIySk9eS"

This is a high-entropy opaque value. The device sends it
on every polling request to identify which authorization session
it is waiting for. It MUST be kept confidential — never shown
to the user. Expires in 1800 seconds (30 minutes).

steps.label: 2. user_code — short human-typeable code
steps.value:
user_code = "WDJB-MJHT"

RFC 8628 §6.1: The user_code SHOULD have high entropy but MUST
be short enough to type on a constrained device (TV remote,
game controller). The AS SHOULD display it in a way that is
easy to distinguish (hyphens, uppercase, no ambiguous chars
like 0/O or 1/l/I).

This code is what the user types on their phone/laptop. It
corresponds to the device_code on the AS side.

steps.label: 3. verification_uri — where the user goes
steps.value:
verification_uri          = "https://auth.example.com/activate"
verification_uri_complete = "https://auth.example.com/activate?user_code=WDJB-MJHT"

The device SHOULD display both:
  • The short URI + user_code (for typing on TV)
  • A QR code encoding verification_uri_complete (for scanning)

verification_uri_complete pre-fills the user_code so the
user can skip the manual entry step.

steps.label: 4. Polling parameters
steps.value:
expires_in = 1800  (seconds until device_code expires)
interval   = 5     (minimum seconds between polling attempts)

The device MUST NOT poll more frequently than once per
interval seconds. If it polls too fast the AS returns
error=slow_down and the device must increase its interval
by 5 seconds.

steps.explanation: The device requests a device code from the AS's device authorization endpoint. The AS returns a device_code (for polling), a user_code (for the user to type), and a verification_uri (where the user goes on their separate device). No user interaction has occurred yet.
annotations: RFC 8628 §3.1: The device authorization endpoint is a separate endpoint from the token endpoint.
RFC 8628 §6.1: user_code entropy should resist brute-force for the duration of the code's lifetime (typically 15–30 minutes).
Public clients (no client_secret) are supported — the client_id alone is sufficient to initiate the flow.
The device_code and user_code are bound together on the AS — when the user approves via the user_code, the AS marks the corresponding device_code as authorized.

steps.label: POST /token (poll — pending)

response_body.error_description: The user has not yet completed authorization. Continue polling.

artifacts.signing_key_hint: Device polls at the interval specified in step 1

steps.label: Device polling loop
steps.value:
The device repeatedly sends token requests using the device_code
as the grant credential. Responses and their meanings:

  authorization_pending  → user hasn't approved yet, keep polling
  slow_down              → polling too fast; add 5s to interval
  access_denied          → user denied; stop polling, show error
  expired_token          → device_code expired; restart from step 1
  200 OK                 → authorized! tokens in response body

The device waits at least `interval` seconds between attempts
(5 seconds in this flow). A 400 with authorization_pending is
expected and normal — it is NOT an error in the failure sense.

steps.explanation: The device starts polling the token endpoint using the device_code as a grant credential. The AS returns authorization_pending because the user has not yet visited the verification URI. The device waits the specified interval before trying again.
annotations: RFC 8628 §3.4: The device MUST wait at least `interval` seconds between polling requests.
RFC 8628 §3.5: error=authorization_pending is the normal response while waiting; the device SHOULD continue polling.
error=slow_down increases the required interval by 5 seconds; the device must respect the new interval on all subsequent requests.
grant_type URI: urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code (must be percent-encoded in form bodies).

steps.label: GET /activate (user navigates + enters code)
steps.explanation: On their phone or laptop, the user navigates to the verification_uri shown by the device and enters (or scans via QR) the user_code. This is out-of-band from the device's perspective — the device does not initiate or observe this request.
annotations: The user_code WDJB-MJHT was displayed by the device (TV screen, CLI output, etc.) in step 1.
verification_uri_complete pre-fills the user_code so a QR scan skips manual entry entirely.
The AS associates the submitted user_code with the pending device_code on the server side. The device never receives or sees this request.

RFC 8628 §3.3: The AS should display the scopes being requested and identify the device/client before asking for consent. Scopes are application-defined permission strings — in this example, `read:profile` grants profile read access, `media:stream` permits streaming content, and `playlists:write` allows the device to create and modify playlists on the user's behalf. The user approves or denies the full set.

steps.label: POST /activate (authenticate + approve)
steps.explanation: The user authenticates and grants consent. The AS marks the device_code corresponding to WDJB-MJHT as authorized for user alice. The next time the device polls, the AS will issue tokens.
annotations: The AS links the approved user_code → device_code → access token grant for user alice.
If the user denies consent, the AS marks the device_code as denied; the next device poll returns error=access_denied.
The device has no visibility into steps 3–4 — it learns the outcome only when its next token poll returns a 200 or error=access_denied.
The AS may show a confirmation screen indicating which device/application was authorized (e.g., 'You have authorized My CLI App').

steps.label: POST /token (poll — success)
steps.explanation: After the user approves on their secondary device, the next device poll returns a 200 with an access token and refresh token. The device's polling loop is complete. The token carries the identity of the user who approved (sub), not the device.
annotations: The access token sub=user_alice_482 identifies the user who authorized on the secondary device, enabling the CLI/device to act on their behalf.
RFC 8628 §3.5: Once tokens are issued, the device_code is consumed and must not be reused.
The device should securely store the refresh_token to obtain new access tokens without requiring the user to re-authorize.
Devices operating on behalf of users should implement token refresh to avoid requiring the user to re-authorize each time the access token expires (every expires_in seconds — 1 hour in this example).

steps.label: GET /api/profile
steps.explanation: The device uses the access token to call the protected API on behalf of the user who authorized on the secondary device. From the RS's perspective, this is an ordinary Bearer token request — the unusual acquisition path is invisible.
annotations: The device flow is transparent to the resource server — the access token is indistinguishable from one obtained via authorization code flow.
The sub claim identifies Alice, not the device. The device is the client acting on Alice's behalf.
For long-lived device sessions (e.g., a TV app), the device should use the refresh token to renew the access token silently without requiring Alice to re-authorize.
